From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Glaciation in North America during the Last Glacial Period

Not to be confused with [Weichselian glaciation](/wiki/Weichselian_glaciation "Weichselian glaciation").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Iceage_north-glacial_hg.png/500px-Iceage_north-glacial_hg.png)](/wiki/File:Iceage_north-glacial_hg.png)

Maximum glaciation of the Northern Hemisphere (black) during the Quaternary climatic cycles

The **Wisconsin glaciation**, also called the **Wisconsin glacial episode**, was the [most recent glacial period](/wiki/Most_recent_glacial_period "Most recent glacial period") of the North American ice sheet complex, peaking more than 20,000 years ago. This advance included the [Cordilleran Ice Sheet](/wiki/Cordilleran_Ice_Sheet "Cordilleran Ice Sheet"), which nucleated in the northern [North American Cordillera](/wiki/North_American_Cordillera "North American Cordillera"); the [Innuitian ice sheet](/wiki/Innuitian_ice_sheet "Innuitian ice sheet"), which extended across the [Canadian Arctic Archipelago](/wiki/Canadian_Arctic_Archipelago "Canadian Arctic Archipelago"); the [Greenland ice sheet](/wiki/Greenland_ice_sheet "Greenland ice sheet"); and the massive [Laurentide Ice Sheet](/wiki/Laurentide_Ice_Sheet "Laurentide Ice Sheet"),[[1]](#cite_note-Harris1997-1) which covered the high latitudes of central and eastern North America. This advance was synchronous with global glaciation during the last glacial period, including the North American [alpine glacier](/wiki/Glacier#Classification_by_size,_shape_and_behavior "Glacier") advance, known as the [Pinedale glaciation](/wiki/Pinedale_glaciation "Pinedale glaciation"). The Wisconsin glaciation extended from about 75,000 to 11,000 years ago, between the [Sangamonian Stage](/wiki/Sangamonian_Stage "Sangamonian Stage") and the current interglacial, the [Holocene](/wiki/Holocene "Holocene"). The maximum ice extent occurred about 25,000–21,000 years ago during the [last glacial maximum](/wiki/Last_glacial_maximum "Last glacial maximum"), also known as the *Late Wisconsin* in North America.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Global_sea_levels_during_the_last_Ice_Age.jpg/330px-Global_sea_levels_during_the_last_Ice_Age.jpg)](/wiki/File:Global_sea_levels_during_the_last_Ice_Age.jpg)

The [Last Glacial Period](/wiki/Last_Glacial_Period "Last Glacial Period") caused a much lower global sea level

This glaciation radically altered the geography north of the [Ohio River](/wiki/Ohio_River "Ohio River"), creating the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes"). At the height of the Wisconsin Episode glaciation, the [ice sheet](/wiki/Ice_sheet "Ice sheet") covered most of [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), the [Upper Midwest](/wiki/Upper_Midwest "Upper Midwest"), and [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England"), as well as parts of [Idaho](/wiki/Idaho "Idaho"), [Montana](/wiki/Montana "Montana"), and [Washington](/wiki/Washington_(state) "Washington (state)"). On [Kelleys Island](/wiki/Kelleys_Island "Kelleys Island") in [Lake Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie"), northern [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey") and in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City")'s [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park"),[[2]](#cite_note-2) the [grooves left in rock](/wiki/Glacial_striation "Glacial striation") by these glaciers can be easily observed. In southwestern [Saskatchewan](/wiki/Saskatchewan "Saskatchewan") and southeastern [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta") a suture zone between the Laurentide and Cordilleran ice sheets formed the [Cypress Hills](/wiki/Cypress_Hills_(Canada) "Cypress Hills (Canada)"), North America's northernmost point that remained south of the continental ice sheets. During much of the glaciation, sea level was low enough to permit land animals, including [humans](/wiki/Humans "Humans"), to occupy [Beringia](/wiki/Beringia "Beringia") (the [Bering Land Bridge](/wiki/Bering_Land_Bridge "Bering Land Bridge")) and move between [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") and [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia"). As the glaciers retreated, glacial lakes were breached in great [glacial lake outburst floods](/wiki/Glacial_lake_outburst_flood "Glacial lake outburst flood") such as the [Kankakee Torrent](/wiki/Kankakee_Torrent "Kankakee Torrent"), which reshaped the landscape south of modern Chicago as far as the Ohio and Mississippi Rivers.

## Timeline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wisconsin_glaciation&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Timeline")]

Two related movements have been termed Wisconsin: Early Wisconsin and Late Wisconsin.[[3]](#cite_note-Sherzer-3): 40–75 The Early Wisconsin was the bigger of the two and extended farther west and south. It retreated an unknown distance before halting. During this period of quiet, the glacial deposits were eroded and weathered. This first Wisconsin period erased all the [Illinoian](/wiki/Illinoian "Illinoian") glacial topography that its glaciers extended over.[[3]](#cite_note-Sherzer-3) The Late Wisconsin ice sheet extended more towards the west than the earlier movements. This may have been due to changes in the accumulation center of the ice sheet, topographic changes introduced by the Early phase or by pressure changes in the ice mass in the north.[[3]](#cite_note-Sherzer-3)

| Table I | |
| --- | --- |
| Table VII – Estimated Age of Glacial Episodes (Leverett) [[3]](#cite_note-Sherzer-3): 74 | |
| Age | Years before Present (YBP) |
| Culmination of Late Wisconsin | 50,000 |
| Culmination of Early Wisconsin | 100,000 |
| Beginning of Wisconsin | 150,000 |
| Culmination of [Illinoian](/wiki/Illinoian "Illinoian") | 300,000 |
| Beginning of Illinoian | 350,000 |
| Culmination of [Pre-Illinoian](/wiki/Pre-Illinoian "Pre-Illinoian"), i.e., old Nebraskan[[4]](#cite_note-Attig-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) | 550,000 |
| Beginning of Pre-Illinoian | 1,200,000 |

## Continental ice sheets

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wisconsin_glaciation&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Continental ice sheets")]

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wisconsin_glaciation&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Laurentide ice sheet")]

### Cordilleran ice sheet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wisconsin_glaciation&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Cordilleran ice sheet")]

Main article: [Cordilleran ice sheet](/wiki/Cordilleran_ice_sheet "Cordilleran ice sheet")

The Cordilleran Ice Sheet has left remnants throughout the [Northern Rocky Mountains](/wiki/Northern_Rocky_Mountains "Northern Rocky Mountains"), covering [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia") and reaching into northern [Washington](/wiki/Washington_(state) "Washington (state)") State and [Montana](/wiki/Montana "Montana"). The Cordilleran ice sheet has more of an Alpine style of many glaciers merged into a whole. The striations made by the ice field in moving over the bedrock show that it moved principally to the west through the passes of the coast range.[[8]](#cite_note-C&S3-8)

### Innuitian ice sheet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wisconsin_glaciation&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Innuitian ice sheet")]

Main article: [Innuitian ice sheet](/wiki/Innuitian_ice_sheet "Innuitian ice sheet")

The Innuitian ice sheet was centered on the [Queen Elizabeth Islands](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_Islands "Queen Elizabeth Islands").

## Formation of proglacial and prehistoric lakes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wisconsin_glaciation&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Formation of proglacial and prehistoric lakes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Glacial_lakes.jpg/500px-Glacial_lakes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Glacial_lakes.jpg)

A diagram of the formation of the Great Lakes

See also: [List of prehistoric lakes](/wiki/List_of_prehistoric_lakes "List of prehistoric lakes")

Whenever the ice sheet melted from the north at a [moraine](/wiki/Moraine "Moraine"), water would begin to pond in the divide between a moraine and the ice front. The ice would act as a dam as water could not drain through the ice sheet, which in the Wisconsin period covered most of the proglacial river valleys. Numerous small, isolated water bodies formed between the moraine and the ice front. As the ice sheet would continue to melt and recede northward, these ponds combined into [proglacial lakes](/wiki/Proglacial_lake "Proglacial lake"). In areas without an available outlet, the water levels would either continue to rise until reaching one or more low spots along the rim of a moraine, or the ice sheet would retreat, opening access to a lower portion of the moraine.[[3]](#cite_note-Sherzer-3): 40–99 Multiple outlets could form through low spots too until one would become dominant after erosion lowered both the outlet and lake surface.

### Meltwater

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wisconsin_glaciation&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Meltwater")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Champlain_Sea_1.png/500px-Champlain_Sea_1.png)](/wiki/File:Champlain_Sea_1.png)

The [Champlain Sea](/wiki/Champlain_Sea "Champlain Sea") - The best evidence of this former sea is the vast clay plain deposited along the [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa_River "Ottawa River") and [St. Lawrence Rivers](/wiki/St._Lawrence_River "St. Lawrence River").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Ice melt and rainfall carried large quantities of [clay](/wiki/Clay "Clay"), [sand](/wiki/Sand "Sand"), and [gravel](/wiki/Gravel "Gravel") from the ice mass. Clays could be moved long distances by moving water, while sand and gravel could not. Thus, sand and gravel [landforms](/wiki/Landforms "Landforms") developed along the sides and front of the ice sheet;[[3]](#cite_note-Sherzer-3) elongated accumulations of this material are known as [kames](/wiki/Kame "Kame"). Mounds along the frontal edge of the ice are called [moraines](/wiki/Moraines "Moraines"). Wherever a subglacial tunnel began infilling, long winding formations known as [eskers](/wiki/Eskers "Eskers") would form. The sweeping plain of sand and gravel beyond the ice margin and a [terminal moraine](/wiki/Terminal_moraine "Terminal moraine") is called an [outwash plain](/wiki/Outwash_plain "Outwash plain") .[[3]](#cite_note-Sherzer-3) The materials left under the glacier when it melts back is called the [ground moraine](/wiki/Ground_moraine "Ground moraine") or [till plain](/wiki/Till_plain "Till plain").[[3]](#cite_note-Sherzer-3) Till is highly permeable and creates a large ground reserve for water. This formation is highly desirable for human economic development as a source of water.[[3]](#cite_note-Sherzer-3)

## Stages of the Wisconsin episode

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wisconsin_glaciation&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Stages of the Wisconsin episode")]

| Table IV | | | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |

| Western Ice | Eastern Ice | Proximate years ago | Position of ice border |
| Mankato | Valders | 25,000 | Northern Washington, Idaho, and Montana to the Continental Divide – north of Edmonton – 65 miles east of Edmonton – northwest corner of North Dakota – Des Moines – west end of Lake Superior – Milwaukee – Port Huron – Buffalo – Schuylerville – St. Johnsbury. |
| (Great reduction of ice) | Cary | 27,500 | Minneapolis – north Wisconsin – south of Chicago – Central Ohio – 50 miles south of Buffalo – Binghamton - Northampton |
| Tazewell | 40,000 | Rockford, Ill. – Peoria – south of Indianapolis – north of Cincinnati – northwestern Pennsylvania – central Long Island |
| Iowan | No known ice | 65,500 | Northern Washington, Idaho, and Montana – northwest North Dakota – east central Iowa - Minneapolis |

## Role in human migration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wisconsin_glaciation&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Role in human migration")]

Prehistoric human migration was likely greatly influenced by this last glacial period, as during much of the Wisconsin era, the formation of a [land bridge](/wiki/Land_bridge "Land bridge") known as [Beringia](/wiki/Beringia "Beringia") across the [Bering Strait](/wiki/Bering_Strait "Bering Strait") is believed to have allowed human occupation of this area which provided potential access for some of the first humans to move between North America and [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia") in [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia") (see [Settlement of the Americas](/wiki/Settlement_of_the_Americas "Settlement of the Americas")). Other human migration routes also opened during interglacial periods in both [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") and Asia.[[11]](#cite_note-Harm2005-11)

## Flora and fauna

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wisconsin_glaciation&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Flora and fauna")]

North American flora and fauna species were distributed quite differently during the Wisconsin era, due to altered temperatures, surface water distribution, and in some cases coverage of earth surface by glaciers. A number of scientific studies have been conducted to determine species distribution, particularly during the Late Wisconsin and early to mid-Holocene. An example of findings is from the investigation of flora species using [pollen core](/wiki/Pollen_core "Pollen core") samples in present-day California. Here in the [Waterman Hills](/wiki/Waterman_Hills "Waterman Hills") researchers found that *[Juniperus osteosperma](/wiki/Juniperus_osteosperma "Juniperus osteosperma")* and *[Pinus monophylla](/wiki/Pinus_monophylla "Pinus monophylla")* were early to mid-Holocene dominant trees, while *[Monardella arizonica](/wiki/Monardella_arizonica "Monardella arizonica")* has been a continuously present understory plant. *[Celtis reticulata](/wiki/Celtis_reticulata "Celtis reticulata")* is an example of a plant present in the early Holocene following Wisconsin glacial retreat, a species no longer present at the [Waterman Mountains](/wiki/Waterman_Mountains "Waterman Mountains") site.[[12]](#cite_note-Hogan2009-12)

* [Ice Age Trail](/wiki/Ice_Age_Trail "Ice Age Trail") – Long-distance hiking trail in the United States
* [Ice core](/wiki/Ice_core "Ice core") – Cylindrical sample drilled from an ice sheet
* [Last Glacial Period](/wiki/Last_Glacial_Period "Last Glacial Period") – Period of major glaciations of the Northern Hemisphere (115,000–12,000 years ago)
* [Retreat of glaciers since 1850](/wiki/Retreat_of_glaciers_since_1850 "Retreat of glaciers since 1850") – Recent shrinking of glaciers due to global warming
* [Timeline of glaciation](/wiki/Timeline_of_glaciation "Timeline of glaciation") – Chronology of the major ice ages of the Earth
* [Younger Dryas](/wiki/Younger_Dryas "Younger Dryas") – Time period c. 12,900–11,700 years ago

### Pleistocene historic names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wisconsin_glaciation&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Pleistocene historic names")]

Historical names of the "four major" glacials in four regions.

| Region | Glacial 1 | Glacial 2 | Glacial 3 | Glacial 4 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Alps** | [Günz](/wiki/Gunz_glaciation "Gunz glaciation") | [Mindel](/wiki/Mindel_glaciation "Mindel glaciation") | [Riss](/wiki/Riss_glaciation "Riss glaciation") | [Würm](/wiki/W%C3%BCrm_glaciation "Würm glaciation") |
| **North Europe** | [Eburonian](/wiki/Eburonian "Eburonian") | [Elsterian](/wiki/Elsterian "Elsterian") | [Saalian](/wiki/Saalian "Saalian") | [Weichselian](/wiki/Weichselian "Weichselian") |
| **British Isles** | [Beestonian](/wiki/Beestonian "Beestonian") | [Anglian](/wiki/Anglian_Stage "Anglian Stage") | [Wolstonian](/wiki/Wolstonian "Wolstonian") | [Devensian](/wiki/Devensian "Devensian") |
| **Midwest U.S.** | [Nebraskan](/wiki/Pre-Illinoian "Pre-Illinoian") | [Kansan](/wiki/Kansan_glaciation "Kansan glaciation") | [Illinoian](/wiki/Illinoian "Illinoian") | [Wisconsinan](/wiki/Wisconsinan "Wisconsinan") |

Historical names of interglacials.

| Region | Interglacial 1 | Interglacial 2 | Interglacial 3 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Alps** | [Günz-Mindel](/wiki/Cromerian_Stage "Cromerian Stage") | [Mindel-Riss](/wiki/Hoxnian_Stage "Hoxnian Stage") | [Riss-Würm](/wiki/Riss-W%C3%BCrm "Riss-Würm") |
| **North Europe** | Waalian | Holsteinian | [Eemian](/wiki/Eemian "Eemian") |
| **British Isles** | [Cromerian](/wiki/Cromerian "Cromerian") | [Hoxnian](/wiki/Hoxnian "Hoxnian") | [Ipswichian](/wiki/Eemian_Stage "Eemian Stage") |
| **Midwest U.S.** | [Aftonian](/wiki/Aftonian "Aftonian") | [Yarmouthian](/wiki/Yarmouthian_Interglacial_(Stage) "Yarmouthian Interglacial (Stage)") | [Sangamonian](/wiki/Sangamonian "Sangamonian") |
